title: 7 Best Poshmark Alternatives in 2025 for Selling Clothes & More
metaDescription: Poshmark's 20% fee hurting your margins? We ranked 7 better alternatives in 2025—including platforms that charge half as much and still sell fast.
heroImageAlt: A neatly organized clothing rack with tags, next to a phone showing a secondhand fashion selling app with items listed for sale
content:
7 Best Poshmark Alternatives in 2025 for Selling Clothes & More

Poshmark has one of the most passionate selling communities in the secondhand world — and one of the most punishing fee structures. Paying 20% on every sale above $15 means a $120 dress nets you $96. Sell $1,000 worth of clothes and you hand Poshmark $200. If that math bothers you (and it should), here are seven alternatives that take less of your money without necessarily taking less of your time.

Quick Comparison: Poshmark vs. 7 Alternatives

Platform Fees Currency Local Pickup Ships Privacy Verdict
Swapedly 10% (max $20) Swap Bucks (1 SB = $1) Yes Yes No social login required 🏆 Best Fee Value
Mercari 10% Cash / card Yes Yes Account required Simple & quick
eBay 13.25% Cash / card No Yes Account required Best for premium items
Depop 10% + payment fee Cash / card No Yes Account required Best for Gen Z fashion
Facebook Marketplace 5% shipped / free local Cash / card Yes Yes FB profile required Good local reach
ThredUp Up to 85% consignment Cash / card No Yes Account required Hands-off consignment
Vinted Free for sellers Cash / card No Yes Account required Best free option

#1 Swapedly — The Poshmark Alternative With a Real Fee Advantage

Let's get specific about what Swapedly saves you. If you sell $500 worth of clothes on Poshmark at 20%, you've handed over $100 in fees. The same sales on Swapedly at 10% capped at $20 per item? Your fees depend on price points, but for a $100 item, you pay $10; for a $200 item, you pay $20. Even across multiple transactions, the savings are substantial.

Swapedly isn't a fashion-only platform — it accepts all secondhand goods — which is actually a feature for most closet clearouts. Your old Lululemon leggings, your barely-used KitchenAid, and your college-era snowboard can all live in the same shop. That flexibility is something Poshmark explicitly doesn't offer.

The Swap Bucks (SB) system is a genuine differentiator. Every sale earns you SB at a 1:1 dollar ratio (minus the 10% fee). Those SB are immediately spendable across the platform. For someone doing regular closet cleanouts, that means every item you sell becomes credit toward items you actually want — a continuous cycle rather than a series of one-off transactions.

New users get 40 Swap Bucks free on signup at swapedly.com. That's $40 in purchasing power before you sell a single thing. Poshmark doesn't offer anything comparable for new sellers.

Unlike Poshmark, Swapedly supports both local pickup and shipping — giving sellers the flexibility to avoid shipping costs entirely for local buyers. The privacy-first account system (no Facebook or social login required) is also meaningfully different.

Swapedly Pros

  • 10% fee capped at $20 — half of Poshmark's 20%
  • $40 Swap Bucks for new users
  • Accepts all categories, not fashion only
  • Local pickup and shipping both available
  • No social media account required
  • Swap Bucks system keeps your proceeds circulating

Swapedly Cons

  • Swap Bucks can't be withdrawn as cash
  • Smaller fashion-specific community than Poshmark

#2 Mercari — The Easiest Poshmark Alternative

Mercari's flat 10% fee is exactly half of Poshmark's, which makes the math easy: you keep more of every sale. The platform isn't fashion-exclusive, which means you can sell clothing alongside everything else you're clearing out. Listing takes a few minutes, shipping labels are integrated, and the buyer experience is clean.

What Mercari lacks compared to Poshmark is community. There are no Posh Parties, no sharing loops, no social mechanics that drive organic discovery. If you have a following on Poshmark, you'd start from zero on Mercari. But if you're a newer seller without established community ties, that's not a real loss.

Mercari Pros

  • 10% fee — half of Poshmark's rate
  • Accepts all categories, not just fashion
  • Simple, fast listing process

Mercari Cons

  • No community features to drive discovery
  • No fee cap — gets expensive over $200
  • Buyer service fees can complicate pricing

#3 eBay — Best for Premium or Designer Fashion

For designer handbags, luxury watches, or authenticated premium clothing, eBay's global audience and authentication program make it the right choice. The Authenticity Guarantee feature for certain categories gives buyers confidence, which translates to higher prices for sellers.

At 13.25%, eBay's fee is lower than Poshmark's 20% on items above $65 or so. For a $300 designer item, eBay takes $39.75; Poshmark takes $60. That difference is real. The complexity and competition on eBay is higher, but so is the buyer pool.

eBay Pros

  • Authenticity Guarantee for luxury items
  • 13.25% fee beats Poshmark on higher-value sales
  • Global buyer reach for premium fashion

eBay Cons

  • 13.25% still higher than Swapedly and Mercari
  • Complex platform — steep learning curve
  • Returns policy heavily favors buyers

#4 Depop — The Gen Z Fashion Alternative

Depop has carved out a devoted niche among younger buyers and sellers, particularly for vintage, streetwear, and fashion-forward pieces. The aesthetic is Instagram-meets-eBay, and the community skews toward people who care genuinely about style rather than just price.

Depop charges 10% plus a payment processing fee, making the effective rate slightly higher than advertised but still well below Poshmark. The platform's reach in the Gen Z demographic is its most distinctive asset — if your inventory fits that aesthetic, you'll find an audience that Poshmark can't match.

Depop Pros

  • Strong Gen Z and streetwear community
  • 10% fee base — half of Poshmark
  • Visual-first listing format drives engagement

Depop Cons

  • Additional payment processing fee adds to effective rate
  • Very trend-specific audience — not good for basics
  • No local pickup option

#5 Facebook Marketplace — Local Clothing Sales, Free

If you're comfortable with local pickup — which works surprisingly well for clothing, especially higher-end pieces where buyers want to inspect before buying — Facebook Marketplace charges nothing for local transactions. No fees, no holds, just cash and a handoff.

The limitation for clothing on Facebook is discoverability. There's no fashion-specific search or community, so you're competing in a general secondhand feed rather than a fashion-focused one. For basics and lower-priced items, this is fine. For styled or curated closets, it's not ideal.

Facebook Marketplace Pros

  • Free for local transactions
  • Large general buyer audience
  • Good for expensive items buyers want to inspect in person

Facebook Marketplace Cons

  • No fashion-specific discovery features
  • Real Facebook identity visible to all buyers
  • Scam accounts are common

#6 ThredUp — The Hands-Off Consignment Option

ThredUp is a different kind of Poshmark alternative — you ship your clothes to them, they sort and list them, and you receive a payout when items sell. The appeal is pure convenience: zero effort listing, zero photography, zero customer service. Just bag it up and mail it.

The fee is brutal though. ThredUp's payout rates can be as low as 15-30% of the sale price for common brands, and they can reject items entirely, sending them to donation. For premium brands, payouts are better. But if you're looking to maximize your return on quality clothing, you'll almost always earn more doing the work yourself on Swapedly or Mercari.

ThredUp Pros

  • Zero effort — they do all the listing and selling
  • Accepts a wide range of women's and kids' clothing
  • Handles shipping both ways

ThredUp Cons

  • Payouts can be as low as 15% — far less than any DIY platform
  • Items can be rejected with no return without paying extra
  • No control over pricing or timing

#7 Vinted — The Free Seller Option for Fashion

Vinted's most distinctive feature is that sellers pay zero fees — nothing. Buyers pay a small service fee, which means the full sale price goes to you. For casual sellers looking to clear out a closet without the 20% Poshmark sting, this is genuinely appealing.

The platform is bigger in Europe than the US, so inventory and buyer density is still building in American markets. Listings are predominantly fashion and accessories. If you're in a major US city, Vinted is worth trying; in smaller markets, the buyer pool may disappoint.

Vinted Pros

  • Zero seller fees — the most generous structure here
  • Growing platform with fashion focus
  • Clean interface, easy listing

Vinted Cons

  • Smaller US buyer base vs. Poshmark or Mercari
  • Fashion and accessories only
  • Limited brand recognition in US markets

Should You Ditch Poshmark?

Not necessarily — if you've built a Poshmark following, that community has real value that doesn't transfer when you switch platforms. But for new sellers or those who haven't gone all-in on Poshmark's community mechanics, the 20% fee is genuinely hard to justify.

Swapedly gives you 10% capped at $20, accepts all categories, supports local pickup, and gives you $40 free to start. For a clothing seller who also has other household items to move, there's no competition. For a pure fashion seller who wants community-driven discovery, mixing Poshmark with a lower-fee platform makes sense.

Curious how Swapedly compares across the board? See our guide to the best Mercari alternatives or the full ranking of the best places to sell stuff online in 2025.
